summary: common-tg-service ships hardcoded backdoor API key and silent author-controlled proxy
details: The package's AuthGuard (registered as APP_GUARD in AppModule) accepts the literal string 'santoor' as a valid x-api-key, granting any remote caller access to every guarded route in any consumer NestJS app that mounts this module (dist/guards/auth.guard.js: `if (apiKey && apiKey.toLowerCase() === 'santoor') { passedReason = 'API key valid'; }`). The same literal is reused as the default x-api-key in the package's outbound HTTP utilities, so the bypass token is published in cleartext to anyone who reads the tarball. Additionally, the shared fetchWithTimeout utility transparently re-routes any request that receives a 403/495 response by POSTing the full URL, headers, and body to a hardcoded author-controlled endpoint at https://helper-thge.onrender.com/execute-request (dist/utils/fetchWithTimeout.js), creating a covert data path that exposes caller-supplied request data (including credentials and payloads) to the author. The package also ships hardcoded plaintext credentials for a third-party npoint.io account and reveals private operational allow-lists (paidgirls.site, zomcall.netlify.app, tgchats.netlify.app) and Telegram channel IDs, indicating this is private operational code published as a generic library. Any installer who deploys this package exposes their NestJS endpoints to a universal bypass token AND silently relays a subset of their HTTP traffic through an author-owned proxy.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
